---
title: |
  Supplementary Material:
  Comparing antigenic distance metrics for influenza
format:
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: project-refs.bib
csl: plos-computational-biology.csl
execute:
  echo: false
  message: false
  warning: false
lang: en-US
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(flextable)

options(knitr.graphics.rel_path = FALSE)

fit_flextable_to_page <- function(tbl, page_width = 6.5) {
	ft_out <- flextable::autofit(tbl)
	ft_width <- (dim(ft_out)$widths * page_width) /
		flextable::flextable_dim(ft_out)$widths
	flextable::width(ft_out, width = ft_width)
}
```

# Reproducibility Instructions

The analysis is organized as a `targets` pipeline. From the repository root,
restore the project environment with `renv::restore()` if needed, then run the
pipeline with:

```r
targets::tar_make()
```

The pipeline defaults to `FLU_TARGETS_MODE=test`, which uses reduced stochastic
settings for development checks. Publication-scale settings are selected by
setting `FLU_TARGETS_MODE=full` before running the pipeline. From an R session,
run:

```r
Sys.setenv(FLU_TARGETS_MODE = "full")
targets::tar_make()
```

From PowerShell, set the environment variable for the current terminal session
before launching R:

```powershell
$env:FLU_TARGETS_MODE = "full"
Rscript -e "targets::tar_make()"
```

The full-mode setting applies only to the current R or terminal session unless
the environment variable is set elsewhere. Full mode uses publication-scale
stochastic and phylogenetic settings: 9,999 Mantel permutations, 4,000 Mantel
bootstrap draws, 9,999 subtype-contrast permutations, 4,000 subtype-contrast
bootstrap draws, 4,000 descriptive-correlation bootstrap draws, 1,000
ML-tree-support bootstrap replicates, and 1,000,000 SH-test resamples. These
settings are expected to run much longer than the default development pipeline.

The Mantel correlation table in the main manuscript is generated from
`cophenetic_mantel_summary`, written to
`results/derived/cophenetic-mantel-summary.rds`, and rendered as
`results/Tables/distance-mantel-table.rds`.
Rank-based Mantel sensitivity results are generated from
`cophenetic_spearman_mantel_summary`, written to
`results/derived/cophenetic-spearman-mantel-summary.rds`, and rendered as
`results/Tables/distance-spearman-mantel-sensitivity-table.rds`.

The raw sequence input is `data/UGAFluVac-sequences.csv`, a protein-sequence
source table with strain names, short names, source databases, accession
numbers, source full-length flags, and hemagglutinin protein sequences. The
pipeline restricts analysis inclusion to H1N1 and H3N2 sequence rows that can
be matched to antigens in the stored cartography maps. Sequence records outside
those stored maps are retained in the source table for provenance but are not
used in the distance comparisons in this manuscript.

# Strain Inclusion and Provenance Audit

The strain audit tables below trace the current analysis panel from the source
sequence table through cartography-overlap inclusion, sequence completeness
checks, and unique pairwise distance comparisons. Source sequence rows absent
from the stored cartography maps are reported as excluded from the current
analysis rather than as missing sequence records.

The analysis panel was expert-selected to span historical H1N1/H3N2 antigenic
space while preserving the ability to compare sequence-derived, temporal,
phylogenetic, and HAI-based cartographic distances. Fluzone vaccine-component
status is defined from the cohort-season vaccine-source table
`data/fluzone_vaccine_strains_by_season.csv`. The high-dose formulation is
classified as trivalent in seasons where any listed component is excluded from
high-dose Fluzone and quadrivalent in seasons where no component is excluded.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-panel-summary-supplement
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Subtype-level summary of the expert-selected H1N1/H3N2 analysis panel,
#|   including isolation-year span, cohort-season Fluzone component status, and
#|   unique off-diagonal pair-count completeness.

here::here("results", "Tables", "panel-summary-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-panel-temporal-coverage
#| fig-cap: |
#|   Isolation-year coverage for included H1N1 and H3N2 panel strains. Points
#|   mark whether the strain was listed as a Fluzone component during one or
#|   more cohort-study vaccine seasons.

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("results", "Figures", "panel-temporal-coverage-plot.png"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-panel-strain-status
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Included analysis-panel strains and cohort-season Fluzone
#|   vaccine-component status.

here::here("results", "Tables", "panel-strain-status-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-fluzone-vaccine-strain-match
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   H1N1 and H3N2 Fluzone vaccine components by season and their matching
#|   status in the H1N1/H3N2 analysis panel.

here::here("results", "Tables", "fluzone-vaccine-strain-match-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-panel-space-coverage
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Available cartographic and phylogenetic distance coverage for the
#|   analysis panel. Distance ranges are summarized from unique off-diagonal
#|   strain pairs.

here::here("results", "Tables", "panel-space-coverage-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-strain-flow-audit
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Flow-style audit of source sequence rows, analysis-panel inclusion,
#|   sequence completeness, and unique pairwise distance counts.

here::here("results", "Tables", "strain-flow-audit-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sequence-source-summary
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Sequence source counts by subtype and analysis-inclusion status.

here::here("results", "Tables", "sequence-source-summary-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-strain-provenance-accessions
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Source database, accession, source and alignment full-length flags, and
#|   inclusion status for each row in the source sequence table.

here::here("results", "Tables", "strain-provenance-accession-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-strain-pair-counts
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Unique off-diagonal pair counts by subtype and distance metric.

here::here("results", "Tables", "strain-pair-count-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Sequence Completeness and Missing-Data Sensitivity

For sequence-derived distances, the primary analysis uses pairwise deletion of
non-comparable aligned positions. In each strain pair, standard amino-acid
residues are comparable; alignment gaps, ambiguous or missing residue codes,
and known nonstandard amino-acid codes are excluded from the relevant
denominator rather than counted as zero-distance substitutions. This matches
the pairwise-deletion behavior commonly used for sequence distances when gaps
or missing states are sparse. As a cheap sensitivity check, we also computed
complete-deletion sequence distances, where sites containing any
non-comparable character in any included strain within subtype are removed
before pairwise distances are calculated.

The complete-sequence sensitivity below removes included strains that are not
full-length by source or alignment audit from the existing distance matrices.
This matrix-subsetting check does not refit the multiple sequence alignment,
maximum-likelihood trees, distance-based trees, or cartography maps. If only
one included strain is non-full-length, the subtype-specific matrix result is
equivalent to the corresponding leave-one-strain-out matrix-removal result, but
the target is reported separately to make the complete-sequence audit explicit.

More complex alternatives, including complete-sequence-only alignment and ML
tree refitting or treating gaps as a separate phylogenetic state, are deferred
unless requested because they answer broader questions and can be substantially
more computationally expensive.

For the matrix-association summaries below, rows are flagged when the absolute
association change is at least 0.10 correlation units, matching the
leave-one-strain-out influence convention.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sequence-character-audit
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Aligned sequence character audit by residue class. Unexpected characters
#|   should be reviewed against source data before publication-scale
#|   interpretation.

here::here("results", "Tables", "sequence-character-audit-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-complete-sequence-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Complete-sequence-only matrix sensitivity after excluding included
#|   strains that are not full-length by source or alignment audit. Estimates
#|   are matrix-association point estimates using existing fitted analysis
#|   objects; alignments, ML trees, and cartography maps are not refit.

here::here("results", "Tables", "complete-sequence-sensitivity-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sequence-missing-data-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Pairwise-deletion versus complete-deletion sensitivity for
#|   sequence-derived distances. Estimates compare each sequence-derived
#|   distance matrix with the existing ML-tree cophenetic distance.

here::here("results", "Tables", "sequence-missing-data-sensitivity-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Secondary Sequence-Distance Sensitivity

The primary whole-sequence amino-acid metric remains Grantham distance, and
the primary epitope-level sequence metric remains $p$-Epitope distance. As
supplementary sensitivity metrics, the pipeline also computes amino-acid
Hamming distance, normalized optimal string alignment distance,
$p$-all-epitope distance, and a BLOSUM62-derived dissimilarity. These metrics
are intended to check whether the qualitative matrix-association pattern is
similar under simpler or alternative sequence distances; they are not part of
the primary four distance metrics.

All secondary sequence distances use the same pairwise-deletion rule as the
primary sequence-derived distances. Hamming distance is the proportion of
comparable aligned amino-acid sites that differ. Optimal string alignment is
computed after pairwise deletion of non-comparable aligned sites and normalized
by the number of comparable sites. $p$-all-epitope uses the same H1N1 and H3N2
epitope site sets as $p$-Epitope but averages the five epitope-specific
Hamming distances rather than taking the maximum. BLOSUM62 dissimilarity is
computed per comparable aligned site as
`S(a,a) + S(b,b) - 2 * S(a,b)` from the BLOSUM62 substitution score matrix,
then averaged across comparable sites.

The secondary matrix-sensitivity table compares each secondary metric's
association with the primary ML-tree cophenetic distance against the closest
primary sequence-distance comparator. Hamming, optimal string alignment, and
BLOSUM62 are compared against Grantham distance; $p$-all-epitope is compared
against $p$-Epitope distance. Rows are flagged when the absolute association
change is at least 0.10 correlation units. These metrics are not used to build
neighbor-joining trees unless full-mode outputs show a major difference or a
coauthor or reviewer requests those topology checks.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-secondary-sequence-distance-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Supplementary secondary sequence-distance sensitivity metrics. Estimates
#|   compare each secondary metric with ML-tree cophenetic distance and with
#|   its closest primary sequence-distance comparator.

here::here("results", "Tables", "secondary-sequence-distance-sensitivity-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Alignment Sensitivity

The primary sequence analysis uses MUSCLE protein alignments through the
`msa` package. As an alignment-method sensitivity check, the pipeline also
computes ClustalW and ClustalOmega protein alignments for the same included
H1N1 and H3N2 strain panels. Nucleotide alignments and nucleotide distances are
not included because the current distance metrics and tree models are based on
hemagglutinin amino-acid sequences.

The matrix-distance sensitivity below recomputes Grantham and p-epitope
distances under each alternative alignment and compares those distance matrices
with the primary MUSCLE ML-tree cophenetic distances. This analysis does not
refit ML trees, distance-based trees, or cartography maps under the alternative
alignments. Rows are flagged when the alternative-alignment estimate differs
from the primary estimate by at least 0.10 absolute correlation units.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-alignment-distance-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Matrix-only alignment sensitivity for sequence-derived distances.
#|   Alternative ClustalW and ClustalOmega distance matrices are compared with
#|   the primary MUSCLE ML-tree cophenetic distances.

here::here("results", "Tables", "alignment-distance-sensitivity-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

The model-selection sensitivity compares FLU-family amino-acid model-test
results across the primary and alternative alignments. A changed selected model
is recorded, but it is only flagged as materially alignment-sensitive when the
primary selected model's log-likelihood loss under the alternative alignment
exceeds the same model-performance tolerance used for the primary
common-versus-subtype model rule.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-alignment-model-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Alignment sensitivity for FLU-family amino-acid model selection. The
#|   primary selected model is flagged only when its loss under an alternative
#|   alignment exceeds the approved model-performance tolerance.

here::here("results", "Tables", "alignment-model-sensitivity-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Tree Model Selection

For ML tree construction, the pipeline compares FLU-family amino-acid
substitution models [@dang2010] with and without invariant sites and discrete
gamma rate variation. In full mode, gamma category counts from 4 to 32 are
evaluated in increments of 4. Within each subtype, the lowest-AICc model is
identified [@burnham2002model]. A common model is preferred for both subtypes
when its log-likelihood loss is no more than 10% relative to the
subtype-specific AICc-supported model. If no common model satisfies this
criterion, the pipeline uses subtype-specific models and the manuscript should
explicitly discuss why subtype-specific model choices were necessary.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-tree-model-selection
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   FLU-family model comparison and selected model rule for ML tree fitting.
#|   Test-mode results use reduced gamma-category settings and should be
#|   regenerated in full mode before final manuscript interpretation.

here::here("results", "Tables", "tree-model-selection-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Antigenic Cartography Input Diagnostics

The cartographic distances in the main analysis are extracted from stored
Racmacs `.ace` maps. These files are treated as first-pass cartography inputs
rather than a full reproduction of the upstream map-building workflow. A
separate manuscript is planned to describe construction of the human-serum
antigenic cartographies in detail. Using the same HAI dataset to construct the
maps and compare cartographic distances is a limitation of the current
analysis, but no comparable alternative is currently available for this strain
panel.

The diagnostics below report map dimensionality, strain and serum counts,
stored stress, stored projection count, and titer-table missingness where these
metadata are extractable from the `.ace` files. Optimization history beyond the
stored stress/projection metadata was not available in the stored `.ace` inputs.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-cartography-diagnostics
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Diagnostics extracted from the stored antigenic-cartography .ace inputs.
#|   Optimization history beyond stored stress and projection count was not
#|   available in these files.

here::here("results", "Tables", "cartography-diagnostics-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Distance-Matrix Comparison Details

The primary manuscript table reports Mantel correlations between ML-tree
cophenetic distance and each candidate distance metric. These tests use only
unique off-diagonal strain pairs. Duplicate symmetric distances and diagonal
self-comparisons are excluded before estimating the observed correlation,
Bayesian bootstrap interval, and matrix-permutation p-value.

All distance-matrix association, subtype-contrast, influence, and
distance-tree analyses use raw distance values unless a result is explicitly
labeled as normalized. Min-max normalized distances are used only for labeled
visual or audit outputs where distances with different units need a common
display scale. The pipeline includes a supplement-ready raw-versus-normalized
handling audit target that can be added here if requested by a reviewer; no
additional normalized sensitivity analysis is reported in this manuscript pass.

For the overall rows, permutation is stratified by subtype: H1N1 and H3N2 labels
are permuted within subtype, then the unique-pair vectors are concatenated for
the overall Mantel statistic. Bayesian bootstrap intervals are resampled over
strain units and are separate from the permutation p-values. We do not report
Bayesian-bootstrap tail probabilities as p-values because the permutation
p-values are the pre-specified apparent-sample test for matrix correspondence.

As a sensitivity analysis for monotonic but nonlinear relationships, we also
compute rank-based Spearman Mantel correlations. These use the same unique
off-diagonal strain-pair representation, subtype-stratified overall
permutation scheme, and strain-level bootstrap interval settings as the primary
Mantel table, but the observed and permuted correlations are computed on ranks.
Rank-based results are reported as sensitivity analyses only; they should not
be used to replace the primary Mantel estimand or to strengthen manuscript
claims without additional human review.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-mantel-settings
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Mantel correlation computation settings. Seeds are row-specific so that
#|   each comparison and reporting scope is reproducible.

here::here("results", "derived", "cophenetic-mantel-summary.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	dplyr::select(
		"Comparison",
		"Scope",
		"Permutations",
		"Bootstrap draws",
		"CI method",
		"Permutation seed",
		"Bootstrap seed"
	) |>
	flextable::flextable() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-mantel-main-copy
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Main Mantel correlation table reproduced for reference.

here::here("results", "Tables", "distance-mantel-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

## Rank-Based Mantel Sensitivity

```{r}
#| label: tbl-spearman-mantel-settings
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Rank-based Mantel sensitivity computation settings. Seeds are row-specific
#|   so that each comparison and reporting scope is reproducible.

here::here("results", "derived", "cophenetic-spearman-mantel-summary.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	dplyr::select(
		"Comparison",
		"Scope",
		"Correlation method",
		"Permutations",
		"Bootstrap draws",
		"CI method",
		"Permutation seed",
		"Bootstrap seed"
	) |>
	flextable::flextable() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-spearman-mantel-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Spearman rank-based Mantel sensitivity table. These results evaluate
#|   monotonic association between each distance metric and ML-tree cophenetic
#|   distance and do not replace the primary Mantel table.

here::here("results", "Tables", "distance-spearman-mantel-sensitivity-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

## Subtype Contrast Analysis

For each distance metric, the primary subtype contrast is defined as the H3N2
Mantel correlation minus the H1N1 Mantel correlation. The Mantel correlations
use the same unique off-diagonal strain-pair representation as the main
distance-matrix table. Uncertainty is estimated with a Bayesian bootstrap over
strain units [@rubin1981]. Within each bootstrap draw, strain weights are
normalized separately within H1N1 and H3N2, pair weights are calculated as the
product of the two endpoint strain weights, and the weighted H3N2-minus-H1N1
contrast is recomputed.

The primary contrast answers a strain-sampling question: how uncertain is the
observed subtype difference if the strains are treated as sampled units from a
broader strain universe? We also report two sensitivity analyses that answer
different questions. The Fisher-z approximation [@fisher1921] treats the
unique strain pairs as independent observations, so it is expected to be
overconfident because each strain contributes to many pairs. The independent
Mantel permutation contrast permutes matrix labels within each subtype and
compares the observed contrast against a joint no-association null; it is useful
as a matrix-aware sensitivity check, but it is not a direct test of equality
between subtype-specific Mantel correlations. Differences among these
summaries can therefore be driven by pairwise non-independence, different
effective sample sizes across subtypes, and the fact that the permutation
sensitivity asks a different null-hypothesis question than the bootstrap
interval.

In practice, the Fisher-z sensitivity should usually look more decisive than
the primary bootstrap interval because it uses the 153 H1N1 and 210 H3N2
pairwise distances as if they were independent observations. The permutation
sensitivity may also differ from the bootstrap interval: for example, it can
flag large temporal or cartographic contrasts against a no-association null
while not flagging smaller contrasts between two already-high subtype-specific
matrix associations.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-subtype-contrast-settings
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Subtype contrast bootstrap settings. Seeds are row-specific so that each
#|   contrast is reproducible.

here::here("results", "derived", "cophenetic-subtype-contrast-summary.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	dplyr::select(
		"Comparison",
		"Bootstrap draws",
		"CI method",
		"Bootstrap seed"
	) |>
	flextable::flextable() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-subtype-contrast-main-copy
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Main subtype contrast table reproduced for reference.

here::here("results", "Tables", "subtype-mantel-contrast-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-subtype-contrast-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Sensitivity summaries for H3N2-minus-H1N1 subtype contrasts. These
#|   alternatives are included to show how the contrast behaves under simpler
#|   Fisher-z and matrix-permutation reference calculations; neither replaces
#|   the primary strain-bootstrap contrast interval.

here::here("results", "Tables", "subtype-mantel-contrast-sensitivity-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

## Descriptive Pearson and Spearman Correlations

The Pearson correlations in @tbl-pearson-correlations are descriptive summaries
of the same unique off-diagonal pair representation used for the Mantel
analysis. They are included to help interpret the approximate linear agreement
between distance scales, but they are not used as independent-observation
inference.

The Spearman correlations in @tbl-spearman-correlations are parallel
descriptive sensitivity summaries for monotonic association. They use the same
strain-level bootstrap interval settings as the Pearson table, with the
correlation statistic computed on ranks. They are reported only to check
whether qualitative patterns depend on the linearity assumption implicit in
Pearson correlation.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-pearson-correlations
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Descriptive Pearson correlations between each distance metric and
#|   ML-tree cophenetic distance, overall and by subtype. Intervals are
#|   strain-level bootstrap intervals and should be interpreted descriptively.

here::here("results", "Tables", "descriptive-pearson-distance-correlation-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-spearman-correlations
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Descriptive Spearman rank correlations between each distance metric and
#|   ML-tree cophenetic distance, overall and by subtype. Intervals are
#|   strain-level bootstrap intervals and should be interpreted as sensitivity
#|   summaries for monotonic association.

here::here("results", "Tables", "descriptive-spearman-distance-correlation-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Leave-One-Strain-Out Influence Analysis

We evaluated strain-level influence on the matrix-association results by
removing one strain at a time within subtype from the existing ML-tree
cophenetic and candidate distance matrices, then recomputing the observed
association estimates on the remaining unique off-diagonal strain pairs. This
analysis does not refit the multiple sequence alignments, ML trees, or
cartography maps; it asks whether the reported matrix associations are driven
by individual strains in the current fitted analysis objects.

Rows are flagged when the absolute leave-one-out change is at least 0.10
correlation units. The derived summary retains both the Mantel-r point estimate
and the descriptive Pearson point estimate. For the within-subtype
leave-one-out comparisons shown here, both point estimates use the same
unique-pair vectors, so the displayed table and figure focus on Mantel r.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-influence-settings
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Leave-one-strain-out influence-analysis settings. The summary retains
#|   both Mantel-r and descriptive Pearson point-estimate rows; the table and
#|   figure below focus on Mantel r.

here::here("results", "derived", "cophenetic-influence-summary.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	dplyr::select(
		"Estimate method",
		"Flag threshold"
	) |>
	dplyr::distinct() |>
	flextable::flextable() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-influence
#| fig-cap: |
#|   Largest leave-one-strain-out changes in Mantel correlation after removing
#|   strains within subtype. Positive values indicate that the estimate
#|   increased after removing the strain; negative values indicate that it
#|   decreased. The complete row-level listing is shown in the influence table.

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("results", "Figures", "leave-one-strain-out-influence-plot.png"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-influence
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Leave-one-strain-out influence on Mantel correlations between each
#|   candidate distance metric and ML-tree cophenetic distance. Changes are
#|   leave-one-out estimates minus full-panel estimates.

here::here("results", "Tables", "leave-one-strain-out-influence-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# Supplemental Tree Topology Checks

The main manuscript reports delta log likelihood as the primary tree-comparison
metric, with SH p-values [@shimodaira1999ShTest] and RF distance
[@robinson1981ComparisonPhylogeneticTrees] as secondary checks. Additional
topology-distance summaries are reported here as sensitivity diagnostics rather
than primary evidence. These metrics are useful for checking whether the
likelihood ranking is accompanied by similar topology-distance patterns, but
they do not replace the likelihood comparison.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-tree-topology-distances
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Supplemental topology-distance metrics between each distance-based
#|   neighbor-joining tree and the optimized ML tree.

here::here("results", "Tables", "tree-topology-distance-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# ML Tree Branch Support and Bootstrap Stability

To evaluate robustness of the optimized ML tree topologies themselves, the
pipeline performs nonparametric site-bootstrap resampling of the fitted ML
object for each subtype. Test mode uses reduced bootstrap settings for pipeline
checks, while full mode should be regenerated before interpreting these values
as publication evidence. The summary below reports branch-support and companion
topology-stability diagnostics. Branch support is the percentage of bootstrap
trees that recover each internal split in the optimized ML tree. The topology
stability summaries compare each bootstrap replicate tree with the optimized ML
tree using RF-style and related tree-distance metrics.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-ml-tree-support
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   ML-tree branch support and bootstrap topology-stability summary. Test-mode
#|   values use reduced bootstrap replicates and should not be interpreted as
#|   final publication-scale robustness evidence.

here::here("results", "Tables", "ml-tree-support-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

The node-level support table is included for auditability. Internal node
numbers refer to the unrooted ML tree object rather than named biological
clades; final clade-level interpretation should use the plotted tree and
full-mode support results together.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-ml-branch-support-detail
#| tbl-cap: |
#|   Internal-node bootstrap support values for the optimized ML trees. Node
#|   identifiers refer to the underlying unrooted tree object.

here::here("results", "Tables", "ml-tree-branch-support-detail-table.rds") |>
	readr::read_rds() |>
	fit_flextable_to_page()
```

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
